main_claims.claim: The number of fundamental dimensionful constants is operationally equivalent to the minimum number of apparatuses required to construct the theory's underlying spacetime.
main_claims.notes: This is a definitional/philosophical axiom that effectively bridges DOV's 'number of fundamental units' with measurable, operational physics.

main_claims.claim: In Galilean spacetime, all physical observables can be expressed strictly in terms of length and time units (MS system) by treating G as a conversion factor.
main_claims.notes: Relies on the historical context of measuring mass via its gravitational parameter GM = aL^2, effectively absorbing the kilogram into meters and seconds.
main_claims.paper_location: Section: Two units to rule us all in Galilei spacetime

main_claims.claim: In Minkowski spacetime, spatial length can be measured exclusively using free-falling clocks, without any reliance on light rays or an a priori defined speed of light.
main_claims.notes: The algebraic and geometric derivation of the Unruh protocol (Eq. 18 and 27) is entirely sound and relies only on the structure of the Minkowski metric.
main_claims.paper_location: Section: Time to rule us all in relativistic spacetimes; Appendix B

missing_assumptions.assumption: Exact validity of the Weak Equivalence Principle is required to eliminate mass as a fundamental unit.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Section: Two units to rule us all in Galilei spacetime
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The argument eliminates the kilogram by defining mass via its gravitational pull (GM = aL^2). If inertial mass and active gravitational mass were not strictly proportional, a separate apparatus/standard would be required to measure the inertial mass used in F=ma.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Universal constancy of coupling constants (like G and the Coulomb constant).
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Section: Recuperating the MKS system from the SI
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: If G varied across the universe, distant labs using strictly kinematic apparatuses (rulers and clocks) to measure mass would obtain inconsistent results when comparing the masses of identical elementary particles. The universality of G is necessary for it to act as a global conversion factor.

novelty_assessment: The paper provides a highly original and clarifying pedagogical perspective on the well-known Duff-Okun-Veneziano controversy. While the idea that c, G, and k_B are mere conversion factors is standard in theoretical physics, formally anchoring the count of fundamental constants to the operational prerequisites for defining the spacetime itself is a fresh, robust approach. The exposition of the Unruh length-measurement protocol is particularly elegant and conceptually valuable.
score_rationale: The paper is conceptually excellent, clearly written, and mathematically sound. It tackles a profound philosophical debate in physics (the number of fundamental constants) and resolves it through a strict, operational framework based on spacetime geometry. The derivations, particularly the Unruh protocol in Appendix B, are flawlessly executed. The authors display strong physical intuition and self-awareness of the limitations (e.g., acknowledging the equivalence principle and quantum limitations on clocks). It is a highly satisfying read.
summary: The paper addresses the Duff-Okun-Veneziano debate regarding the number of fundamental constants by proposing an operational framework rooted in spacetime construction. The authors argue that the number of fundamental constants is equivalent to the minimum number of independent apparatuses required to define the underlying spacetime. They demonstrate that Galilean spacetime necessitates two apparatuses (rulers and clocks), reducing the necessary units to meter and second by absorbing mass via the gravitational constant. In relativistic spacetimes, they employ a clever purely clock-based length measurement protocol (credited to Unruh) to show that only one apparatus (a clock) is required to define the spacetime and express all physical observables. Consequently, they conclude that the number of fundamental constants in relativistic physics is exactly one.

technical_objections.evidence: In a curved spacetime with non-zero Weyl curvature (tidal forces), freely falling objects will undergo geodesic deviation. A clock cannot remain both in pure free-fall (inertial) and precisely trace the spatial profile of a rigid rod unless lateral constraint forces are applied, which technically breaks pure inertiality.
technical_objections.objection: The assumption of strictly 'inertial' (freely falling) clocks moving 'along the rod' in Unruh's protocol is physically problematic in general relativistic spacetimes with tidal forces.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section: Time to rule us all in relativistic spacetimes; Appendix B
